svg.text: 13.5 · A backup you can't restore is NOT a backup — it's a hope
backups rot silently; you discover it DURING the disaster (the worst time) — only a tested RESTORE proves recoverability
THE TRAP — "backup succeeded" ✓ every night
...for a year. But it silently:
✗ is CORRUPT (bit-rot / a backup bug)
✗ is INCOMPLETE (a table excluded)
✗ is UN-DECRYPTABLE (lost the key, 13.14)
✗ is MISSING the binlog (no PITR, 13.3)
→ discovered DURING the disaster = data gone
AND the recovery gone (M15)
THE ANTIDOTE — automated restore DRILL
① restore the backup to a SCRATCH instance
② apply binlog PITR to a target (13.3)
③ VERIFY — row counts, checksums,
RECONCILE (balance = Σ entries, M12/12.14)
④ measure restore time vs RTO (13.4)
→ run it NIGHTLY, alert on any failure
the deliverable is RECOVERABILITY, not backups — and it's only real if you've TESTED the restore
the drill catches EVERY silent failure (corruption, incompleteness, key loss, missing binlog, RTO violation) BEFORE the disaster
"you don't have backups — you have restores" · for money: automate the drill + reconciliation → PROVE recoverability nightly
the operational embodiment of "verify, don't assume" (same discipline as monitoring the guarantee M10/10.12, reconciliation M12/12.14)
⚠ the backup that "ran successfully" but couldn't restore when needed = THE M15 cautionary tale
